1: "Not to us, O LORD — not to us — but to your name give glory, because of your steadfast love and your faithfulness" — the foundational doxological posture: glory belongs to YHWH, not to human agents. Jesus lived this perfectly: "I do not accept glory from human beings" (John 5:41); "I am not seeking glory for myself; but there is one who seeks it, and he is the judge" (John 8:50). John 17:4: "I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do." The prayer of Psalms 115:1 — that glory go to the divine name — is the prayer Christ embodies in his entire life and death.
2: "Why should the nations say, 'Where is their God?'" — the taunt of the nations when Israel suffers. This same taunt surrounded Christ's crucifixion: "He trusts in God. Let God rescue him now if he wants him, for he said, 'I am the Son of God'" (Matthew 27:43). The scoffers at Calvary ask exactly the question of Psalms 115:2 — where is God for this one? The answer is the resurrection: God vindicates his own by raising Christ, answering every taunt with the power of new life (Acts 2:24).
3: "But our God is enthroned in the heavens; he does whatever he pleases" — the sovereign freedom of YHWH. Jesus in Gethsemane: "not what I will, but what you will" (Mark 14:36) — the sovereign pleasure of the Father is the will Christ submits to, even when that pleasure includes the cross. Acts 4:28: "They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen" — the events of the passion were within the sovereign pleasure of YHWH. The God who does whatever he pleases does his most sovereign act in the cross and resurrection.
4: "Their idols are silver and gold — nothing more than what human hands have made" — the idol polemic. Colossians 1:15: Christ is "the image of the invisible God." The idols are humanly manufactured images; Christ is the divinely given image. Acts 17:29: "We should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone — an image made by human design and skill." The idol critique of Psalms 115:4-8 points toward the need for the true image — the one not made by human hands but given by God in the Incarnation.
5: "They have mouths but cannot speak; they have eyes but cannot see" — the impotent idol. In contrast, Christ speaks with divine authority (Matthew 7:29), sees with divine perception (John 2:25: "he knew what was in each person"), and hears with divine compassion (John 11:35). The catalogue of idol-failures in Psalms 115:5-7 is a negative image of everything Christ positively is: the living one who speaks, sees, hears, acts — the true image of the living God (Colossians 1:15).
6: "They have ears but cannot hear; they have noses but cannot smell" — the idol cannot perceive. Jesus "heard" every cry — the blind Bartimaeus (Mark 10:47-48), the desperate Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:22-28), the leper's cry (Mark 1:40). Isaiah 50:4-5: "The Sovereign LORD has opened my ears" — the Servant's open ear is the opposite of the idol's deaf silence. Christ is the Servant with an ear always open to the human cry.
7: "They have hands but cannot feel anything; they have feet but cannot walk; they produce no sound from their throat" — the idol is totally unresponsive. Jesus touches the leper (Mark 1:41: "filled with compassion, Jesus reached out his hand and touched the man"), walks toward the suffering (Matthew 14:25-26), and raises his voice in the temple (John 7:37). The totally unresponsive idol of Psalms 115:7 is the foil for the fully responsive incarnate Lord who heals through touch, moves toward the hurting, and speaks words of life.
8: "Those who make them will end up like them — and so will everyone who puts their trust in them" — the moral conformity principle: worshipers become like what they worship. Second Corinthians Psalms 3:18: "we all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory, which comes from the Lord, who is the Spirit." The inverse of the idol-maker's deformation is the Christ-worshiper's transformation — those who trust the living God in Christ are progressively conformed to the image of the Son (Romans 8:29).
9: "Israel, put your trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the first of three calls to trust, covering Israel, Aaron, and all who fear. John 14:1: "Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me" — Jesus explicitly extends the trust-in-YHWH call to himself. The help-and-shield formula for YHWH becomes the description of what Christ is for his people: "the Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid" (Hebrews 13:6, quoting Psalms 118:6) and the "shield of faith" (Ephesians 6:16) that comes through trust in him.
10: "House of Aaron, put your trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the priestly house called to trust. Christ is the great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14) who fulfills and supersedes the Aaronic priesthood — and he is also the one in whom Aaron's house now trusts for access to God. Hebrews 7:11: "If perfection could have been attained through the Levitical priesthood... why was there still need for another priest?" The House of Aaron who trusted YHWH ultimately trusted forward toward the one who would be their perfect high priest.
11: "All who fear the LORD, put your trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the universal call beyond Israel and the priesthood to all who fear God. Acts 10:34-35: "God does not show favoritism but accepts from every nation the one who fears him and does what is right." The inclusion of all God-fearers in the call of Psalms 115:11 is the opening toward the Gentile mission that Christ commissions (Matthew 28:19-20). All who fear YHWH from every nation are now called to put their trust in the Lord Christ — the widest possible invitation.
12: "The LORD has remembered us and he will bless us; he will bless the house of Israel; he will bless the house of Aaron" — the divine memory as the basis of blessing. Luke 1:54-55: Mary sings that God "has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful to Abraham and his descendants forever." God's remembering in the Incarnation is the fulfillment of the remembering that Psalms 115:12 announces. The blessing of Israel and Aaron's house flows from the divine memory of the covenant — and the blessing of all nations flows from the covenant fulfilled in Christ.
13: "He will bless all who fear the LORD, the small as well as the great" — no status distinction in the blessing. Luke 1:52: "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble." Matthew 18:4: "whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven." Christ's inversion of social hierarchy — the small blessed as well as the great — is embedded in the Hallel he sings at Passover on the night of his arrest.
14: "May the LORD grant you increase — you and your children!" — the blessing of generational increase. John 12:24: "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit." The increase of Psalms 115:14 is ultimately the fruitfulness of the resurrection — Christ's death produces many children (Hebrews 2:13 quotes Isaiah 8:18: "Here am I, and the children God has given me"). The divine grant of increase is the multiplication of the church through the gospel of the risen Christ.
15: "May you be blessed by the LORD — he who made both heaven and earth!" — the Creator's blessing. Genesis 14:19: "Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth" — the Melchizedek blessing that Christ fulfills as the eternal priest (Hebrews 7:1-3). The blessing from the Creator-God echoes through the Melchizedek tradition and arrives in Christ the priest-king who mediates the Creator's blessing to all Abraham's children (Galatians 3:29: "if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise").
16: "The highest heavens belong to the LORD, but the earth he has given to the children of mankind" — the eschatological land-grant. Matthew 5:5: "Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth." The earth "given to the children of mankind" in Psalms 115:16 points toward the eschatological inheritance of the new earth, which the Beatitudes promise to the meek — those who are heirs because they are in Christ the true Heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2).
17: "The dead cannot praise the LORD, nor can any who descend into the silence of the grave" — the pre-resurrection horizon of the psalmist. Christ's resurrection directly shatters this limitation: Acts 2:31: "he was not abandoned to the realm of the dead, nor did his body see decay." Revelation 5:9-13: every creature, including the formerly dead raised in Christ, sings to the Lamb — the dead who could not praise in Psalms 115:17 become the dead who rise to praise, because Christ has broken the silence of the grave.
18: "But we will praise the LORD from this moment on, now and forever. Praise the LORD!" — the vow of perpetual praise. Revelation 22:3-5: "His servants will serve him... and they will reign for ever and ever." The "from now and forever" of Psalms 115:18 is the eternal praise of the new creation — a praise that will never end because the one praised lives and reigns forever. The Hallel's closing promise of perpetual praise is the preview of the eternal worship scene in Revelation before the Lamb.
